item.title: Lambda Calculus, Combinatory Logic, and Type Theory
item.description: the lambda calculus something that i have noticed about the lambda calculus is the fact that it is very simple given a programming background; but is full of strange and mysterious names for simple concepts that we programmers see in everyday life. i create these notes as a reference to some of the notations and concepts present within the lambda calculus, as well as some supplementary material such as theorems and definitions.
item.content:
the lambda calculus

something that i have noticed about the lambda calculus is the fact that it is very simple given a programming background; but is full of strange and mysterious names for simple concepts that we programmers see in everyday life.

i create these notes as a reference to some of the notations and concepts present within the lambda calculus, as well as some supplementary material such as theorems and definitions.

lambda calculus is a formal system of logic which aims to describe computation. we can prove that the system is indeed turing complete via the church-turing thesis, which states that; any problem which is computable by the lambda calculus.

the relation between the lambda calculus and turing machine can be formally defined via the church turing thesis, which states what any problem which can be effectively calculable in finite time by a turing machine, can be described via the lambda calculus. (i am sort of paraphrasing, so apologies if it is not as correct as the original definition of the thesis).

we know this term simply as turing completeness, and its a trait that is demonstrated within the majority, if not all programming languages shown today. so by definition, the lambda calculus is technically a bare minimum for a programming language. (with the exception of smaller formal systems such as iota/jot/zot).

basic syntax

the basic syntax of the lambda calculus is as follows

<term> -> <variable>
      | λ <variable>. <variable>
      | ( <term> <term> )

the syntax for the system of logic is so incredibly simple, but as we will see later; it holds the computational power equivalent to that of the turing machine (via the church-turing thesis).

disclaimer

before we proceed, something that i should inform you of is the notation that i will be using throughout the rest of the post. i am not going to be strictly sticking with the syntax, and there will be slight deviations as i will mention now.

  • when defining a lambda abstraction, i will not be using the lambda character. instead, i will be using haskell’s style of defining lambda functions, and simply represent the λ character by a backslash \, followed by the variable.
  • i will omit useless braces at various points within my notes if they are not necessary.

currying

as we saw from the previous bnf definition of the syntax of lambda calculus, every application must only contain a single variable. this means that we cannot pass more than one parameter to a function/ abstraction within the lambda calculus.

for example, here is an invalid function abstraction which performs addition.

\xy.(x + y)

lets curry this function and correct it.

\x.(\y.(x + y))

very nice, now we have a valid abstraction.

free and bound variables | closure & combinators

a bound variable is a variable which is declared within the current function scope.

on the other hand, a free variable is a variable which utilizes a variable from a parent scope. within programming, this can be thought of as using a global variable, or a lambda capturing a variable from a parent scope. in this case, the function can be thought of as a closure.

this may seem rather arbitrary, but some rather interesting complications arise as we begin to work with higher order functions. if we can have closures which inherit scope from a parent, what happens when we return this function? the closure is no longer present underneath the parent scope, so does the scope go with the closure, or does it simply cause an error?

within a lexically scoped language, the closure will keep its scope, and it’s free variables. this may differ in some languages, but it remains true in the lambda calculus.

a combinator on the other hand is the complete opposite, in the sense that it does not use any free variable. this is the only requirement of a combinator. the concept of a combinator can be further elaborated on within the study of combinatory logic (not to be confused with combinatorics).

there is going to be a section for combinatory theory at a later point within the blog. the only thing we need to understand at this point is the concept of the combinator/closure, and how they relate to free/bound variables and higher order functions.

beta reduction

the concept of beta reduction is simple. we simply apply a variable to an abstraction, which we can do simply by substituting. this is simply a fancy name for a very very well understood concept within the rudimentary algebras.

here is a small demonstration of the beta reduction.

(\x.x) 3

we can reduce this simply by substituting the redex by 3, which results in 3.

3

now that was a fairly simple example of beta reduction, lets amp this up a bit and write a lambda abstraction which simply performs addition on the natural numbers.

(\x.(\y.(x + y)) 10) 20

now lets apply a beta reduction to this step by step and view the result.

1. (\x.(\y.(x + y)) 10) 20
2. (\y.(20 + y)) 10
3. (20 + 10)
4. 30

now as we can see, beta reduction simply means substituting the variables within the abstractions.

the church-rosser theorem

when applying variables to lambda abstractions, we also need to know the order in which we are reducing/evaluating the abstraction.

the church rosser theorem states that, “when applying reduction rules to terms, the ordering in which the reductions are chosen does not make a difference to the eventual result”. this theorem essentially states that the lambda calculus is confluent.

so if the result of the abstraction is always the same, then why is the evaluation order/strategy significant?

the church rosser theorem states that all abstractions will always evaluate to the same result regardless of the strategy, but something to note is the fact that not all evaluation strategies will result in a final outcome.

so the theorem states that there is no logical fallacy within the lambda calculus, as it is confluent and will produce the same result after evaluation, but not all evaluation strategies will produce a correct result.

this is where the importance of reduction strategies lie, as we must use the correct order if we want to produce a correct final result.

here is a diagram of the proof for the theorem. this is much easier to visualize as opposed to the textual proof.

this proof of the church rosser theorem directly translates to, “if there are two distinct reductions or sequences of reductions that can be applied to the same term, then there exists a term that is reachable from both results, by applying (possibly empty) sequences of additional reductions”.

beta normal form

beta equivalence

alpha conversion

alpha equivalence

eta conversion

eta reduction

encoding types

untyped lambda calculus by itself is, as the name suggests, inherently untyped. this means that there are no natural numbers, booleans, strings; no types of any kind. however, we can encode types within the untyped formal system, which we can then use to perform calculations and logic.

within the previous demonstrations, i made extra effort in ensuring that i have explicitly specified that the lambda absractions that we were working with were made under the assumption of natural numbers.

church encoding

church numerals

church booleans

church predicates

church pairs

church lists

church encoding demonstration

i find the concept of encoding types within an untyped language particularly interesting, so i have decided to further demonstrate this concept by encoding each monomorphic church type within haskell; along with their associated operations.

scott encoding

scott encoding works under the same concept as the church encoding (which was previously demonstrated).

peano numbers

combinatory calculi

combinatory calculi is rooted in the lambda calculus, but extends the system of logic by defining such things called combinators. we have previously touched on the concept of a combinator within a previous section of these notes, and the definition remains the same within combinatory calculi.

as previously stated, a combinatory term is simply a function that contains no free variables.

the fixed point theorem

a fixed point of a function is a value that is mapped to itself by the function. in lambda calculus, the same concept applies in the sense that we can apply the same abstraction by itself, which ultimitely results in something known as the fixed point combinator. the fixed point combinator, also known as the Y combinator; is a means of defining recursion within the lambda calculus.

the Y combinator can be effectively defined as follows.

bohm’s theorem

quasi leftmost reduction theorem

recursion and computation

curry howard isomorphism

simple typing

subtyping

recursive typing

system F and polymorphism

the hindley milner type system

hindley milner type inference

now we are treading into the territory of compiler frontend development with the type inference algorithm of a hindley milner type system. this is an algorithm that it utilizes within a large sum of languages, most notable haskell. this is only required within a statically typed language, which is a language which requires that all types are infered and declared at compile time, so they do not cause problems at runtime.

there are always tradeoffs with every decision within all fields of sciences, so i will attempt to clarify the definitions of programming type systems, as well as the benefits that are inherit within each.

higher order type systems

the lambda cube

static typing

the concept of static typing, as previously stated, is the act of the compiler inferring and handling the type checking at compile time; in the case of programming languages, the frontend compiler.

language such haskell, C, C++, java, rust and more use this; and is commonly seen within languages which compile to machine code, as opposed to some intermediate representation which acts as bytecode for some abstract runtime environment.

the following is a list of the benefits that i can currently think of for statically typed languages.

  • faster runtime spees as the environment does not have to worry about type checking.
    • this remains true for both interpreted and compiled languages.
  • a must for compiling to machine code, as hardware does not support type checking.
  • it makes bugs easy to catch early on, as the compiler can warn the user and terminate compilation.
  • there are probably much more, but my brain is fuzzy right now. sorry uwu.

dynamic typing

a dynamically typed language is a language which performs type checking at runtime. this approach to type checking is only present within interpreted languages, since as previously stated; hardware has no support for type checking within its runtime. it only understand data and code, information and opcodes.

now before we get into the pros of dynamic typing, i want to first explain an extremely important concept within dynamic typing that almost all runtime environment use; and that is call ducktyping.

the concept of ducktyping is very simple. there is a funny little joke that is used that encapsulates this concept fairly well, and serves as the origin of the name: “if it walks like a duck, and quacks like a duck, then it must be a duck”.

this may seem a bit strange and arbitrarily random at first glance, but i will attempt to explain the meaning behind this statement. lets first start off with an example.

within python, everything is stored as an object at runtime. these include literally everything, from integers, lists, strings, functions, classes, modules; everything that is a tangible entity at runtime will be stored as an object. these objects themselves hold their own type information. this allows the object’s type to be changed at runtime dynamically simply by assigning it a different type and values.

when the python runtime want to evaluate something as simple as an expression, it will first need to ensure that the types within the expression are capable of arithmetic. it does do this by categorizing every single type into what it can and cant do, that would be way too inefficient for everybodies standards.

instead, it will simply check to see if the object is capable of the arithmetic operation. it doesnt care about its type at all, it simply check if the object is capable of the operation it wants to do. if the object looks like an integer that can perform arithmetic, then the interpreter will attempt to perform arithemtic on the object.

this drastically speeds up the the type checking, and by product the runtime speeds. i will explain the entire process of the type checking system at a later section within the post, if you are interested then you can skip there directly. there are no additional prerequisite materials for that section of the post besides a fairly general understanding of the c programming language.

the benefits of dynamically typed languages are as shown.

  • lack of type checking pass makes compilation speeds faster.
    • this is generally a massive benefit for scripting languages such as python and lua.
  • dynamic types allow for much faster object oriented runtime polymorphism.
  • generic programming does not have to be restricted at compile time.

strongly typed

now we move onto the concept of strongly and weakly typed systems. something that is very important to note is the fact that these two have NO RELATION TO STATIC/DYNAMIC TYPING!!!! this is something that had confused me when i had originally come across these concepts.

the concept of strongly typed languages simply means that the language itself enforces a restriction on converting/performing operations between types. in this case, it simply means that an integer cannot perform an operation with a float if they are defined as two different types. languages such as haskell and C++ are strongly typed.

this may seem like a very odd and restrictive decision to emplace on a type system for a programming language, but it does come with it’s benefits and solutions.

for example, strongly typed languages will generally include something within their langauge known as ad-hoc or bounded polymorphism. this is when the user may explicitly state what function can operate on which types. i understand that this definition of ad-hoc polymorphism may be a bit vague, but i will do my best to explain this to the best of my capacity.

as you may know, C++ is not exactly the best example to show strong typing, as its foundations are built entirely off of the C programming language, which contains a concept known as implicit conversion. this allows for explicit type casting, and automatic conversions within expressions for a much more streamlined programming experience. for this reason, i will be using haskell to explain this concept.

haskell is a purely functional, statically and strongly typed language which is largely rooted in mathematics. this makes it a very beautiful language, but also a pain to code in. some people may disagree with this statement, but i believe that it is an irrefutable fact that there are better languages that are specifically tailored to the programming experience; as opposed to the purity of the language itself. an example of this is ocaml, but that another rant that i dont want to get into here.

within haskell, there is a concept known as a typeclass. this is a feature that allows you to define a finite set of methods that can operate across multiple types. if you don’t have a background in any functional language, i would highly recommend learning one. it will greatly aid in your understanding of the concepts present within the lambda calculus, which functional programming is largely based on. (i say largely, as some functional languages are not pure).

for those who have experience in C++, ad-hoc/bounded polymorphism is the same thing as operator overloading. operator overloading allows the user to overload functionality of methods/functions on specific types. this is an important staple within generic programming in C++.

the following is a list of the benefits of a strongly typed system.

  • type correctness within the program
    • if the programmer is forced to explicitly defined the set of operations which are allowed on a set of types, then the programmer will know that the expressions are indeed using the correct types; as opposed to allowing the compiler to perform implicit type casts.
  • static analysis is much more useful within a strongly typed languages as the analyzer knows each  type of each operand within an expression.
  • eliminates an entire class of bugs/vulnerabilities that are present within weakly typed language, such as type confusion and insecurity.

weakly typed

a weakly typed language is the opposite of a strongly typed language (wow really?), in the sense that the language does not enforce operations between exact types; and may allow for explicit conversion between types.

an example of a weakly typed language is the C programming language (semi-weakly typed). i chose C for this example, as an extremely well known concept within the language is type casting and implicit conversion (as we covered previously). this allows the programmer to explicitly cast the types present within an expression to satisfy the compiler.

this approach places much more freedom within the hands of the programmer and reduces the amount of code they have to write for semi-generic code, but also introduces a class of vulnerability into the language. if used incorrectly, the type safety of the program may potentially be compromised, and may cause undefined behaviour in the best case, and an exploitable bug in the worst.

however, weakly typed languages are not without benefits, as with all tradeoffs. the following is a list of the benefits of weak typing.

  • places much more control in the hands of the developer and allows them to explicitly convert types.
    • this largely depends on the language specification and compiler.
  • reduces code bloat and boiler-plate.
    • the user does not have to explicitly define the operations to be performed on each type to achieve ad-hoc/bounded polymorphism

python’s type checking system

conclusion

welp thats it for my notes on lambda calculus. if youve made it this far then i hope you managed to get something out of this clusterfuck of information. i tried my best to properly format and structure these notes whilst i was taking them, so hopefully they were semi-readable.

if theres any information that was incorrect within these notes, please do me a favour and inform me via one of my contacts listed in my about page. i am always willing to learn more, and if i have misunderstood a concept throughout my process of self study, it would be a massive benefit to be corrected of the misconception.

thanks again for reading. have a great day uwu.

resources

resources and references that were used in the making of my notes.

lambda calculus

  • https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/lambda-calculus/
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lambda_calculus
  • https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/65622/whats-the-point-of-eta-conversion-in-lambda-calculus
  • https://stackoverflow.com/questions/55996764/lambda-eta-conversion-expansion-reduction-clarification
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Church%E2%80%93Rosser_theorem
  • https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33509954/explaining-the-church-rosser-theorem-in-basic-terms
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Church_encoding

combinatory calculi

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Combinatory_logic
  • https://www.amazon.com/Lambda-Calculus-Combinators-Introduction-Roger-Hindley/dp/0521898854
  • https://encyclopediaofmath.org/wiki/Combinatory_logic

type theory (not homotopy type theory (HoTT) )

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lambda_cube
  • https://users.cs.northwestern.edu/~jesse/course/type-systems-wi18/type-notes/index.html
  • http://www.mathemafrica.org/?p=13035
  • https://www.amazon.com/Types-Programming-Languages-MIT-Press/dp/0262162091

item.title: Haskell
item.description: preface something really cool about purely functional languages is the amount they can achieve given such a simple formal system. by the church-turing thesis, a function which operates on natural numbers can be calculated by an effective method if and only if it is computable by a turing machine; therefore rendering a simple system like LC turing complete. amazing.. all operators within haskell are apparently also functions, which is something i find very fascinating due to the fact that it allows us to define our own as well.
item.content:
preface

something really cool about purely functional languages is the amount they can achieve given such a simple formal system. by the church-turing thesis, a function which operates on natural numbers can be calculated by an effective method if and only if it is computable by a turing machine; therefore rendering a simple system like LC turing complete. amazing.. all operators within haskell are apparently also functions, which is something i find very fascinating due to the fact that it allows us to define our own as well. fp is cool!

laziness

a feature of haskell is laziness, which is often praised as a great feature of the language due to the fact that it provides a plethora of benefits that i will explain. the term lazy implies to the fact that nothing is evaluated unless it has been explicitly applied. you could define a function which theoretically falls into infinite recursion; but wont due the fact that only the required values will be computed.

this laziness allows for elegant solutions to problems which would otherwise require much lengthier code to solve.

naming conventions

all types within haskell must be capitalized. this is not a convention, it is enforced by the language. this allows the developer to encode semantics within the source, which therefore leads to more readable code (and technically faster compilation).

type system

haskell operates on the hindley milner type system, which is essentially a restricted system F which requires annotations. this means that haskell has things like polymorphism and type inference within its system.

within purely functional languages, almost everything is an expression, and all values are immutable by default. this includes things like conditionals, which are not technically statements but ternary expressions (conditional expressions). the only exception to which procedural statements are allowed within haskell, is via the “do” keyword. this allows us to sequentially define statements until the end of the function definition.

polymorphism

within simply typed lambda calculus, types are monomorphic, with the term mono meaning singular. each type must represent T -> T. This is not the case with system F, and by product hindley milner. haskell features 2 forms of polymorphism within the language, parametric and ad-hoc/bounded.

parametric polymorphism applies to functions which works on many different types. another term for this is polymorphic functions. an example of this is the length function within the Prelude module. no matter the type of the value stored within the list, it will always properly apply.

the function signature for the length function will look something of the sort: length :: forall a. [a] -> Integer

this means that “for all types a, the function length takes a list of elements of type a and returns an integer. haskell does not require us to explicitly use the forall function, as that would lead to fairly ugly, unreadable, and unmaintainable code fairly quickly. instead, we can define a polymorphic function as such: foo :: a -> Int foo c = 10

the function accepts any type as an argument, but will return only an integer. this ensures that we are able to place semantic restrictions upon the types passed to our polymorphic function. of course, there are much better means of applying this feature within our type system.

integer types within haskell are polymorphic too. haskell has 4 type classes for handling integral types, Int, Integer, Float, and Double. the difference between Int and Integer, is that the Integer has arbitrary precision.

ad-hoc or bounded polymorphism applies to the actual operations that are performed on types. an example of this is C++’s operator overloading, which allows the user to write and rewrite the operations in which operators perform depending on the type. this is vital for generic programming, due to the fact that you could reuse a generic algorithm as long as the operators for the type/class had been properly implemented.

this form of polymorphism has been implemented within haskell as well, in the form of type classes. with these type classes, we can define our own types, and the actions that operators within the language will perform on these types. this is possible since operators within haskell are also functions, as previously stated.

important thing to note, the let keyword allows us to define variables within expressions. this is the usage of the keyword, as it was a bit confusing as to why this existed when you could leave it out when defining variables normally.

definition: higher order functions

higher order functions are functions which work with functions. this means that they can either have functions as parameters, or return functions as results. we can encode programming idioms, parsers, or algebraic properties within higher order functions.

okay so rank n types seem to be a higher rank of parametric polymorphism, in the sense that we can apply the forall polymorphic attribute to the signature of our function. this allows our polymorphic function to operate with polymorphic functions.

im having some trouble understanding exactly why this is necessary, or why this isnt part of the core language; and instead delegated to an extension? is this just a ghc specific feature not present within the standard?

yea its only a ghc extension lol. there should be some pattern to achieve the same outcomes no? else this would be present within standard haskell as well.

something that i had not realized before was the fact that untyped lambda calculus quite literally is untyped. in all the demonstrations that had been shown to me, plenty of them had been utilizing natural numbers within their demonstrations. i now realize that those are church numerals, and you can literally encode types within an untyped language. very cool.

however i still dont understand why untyped lambda calculus is technically more “powerful” than simply typed lambda calculus. i get the fact that you can manually encode all types in the typed LC without the notation within the formal system, but that does not exactly explain why it is deemed more powerful. i have not found a resource that explains this as of yet.

item.title: About
item.description: Hi I’m xcr0. My interests include programming, compiler development, theoretical computer science, vulnerability research, reverse engineering, and ctf. You can find me via any of the contacts listed below. Matrix: @xcr0:matrix.org Keybase Github Twitter Email PGP: CC142B6F68880A9458136676D3DCCFFC5ED57822
item.content:
Hi I’m xcr0. My interests include programming, compiler development, theoretical computer science, vulnerability research, reverse engineering, and ctf.

You can find me via any of the contacts listed below.

  • Matrix: @xcr0:matrix.org
  • Keybase
  • Github
  • Twitter
  • Email
  • PGP: CC142B6F68880A9458136676D3DCCFFC5ED57822

item.title: Decompilers
item.description: wip… preface a large reason as to why i wanted to create a collection of notes, information, and references on decompilers is primarily due to the fact that its such an obscure craft. i have personally never known of any class of software that was as difficult to find resources on as decompilers. hell even the documentation for open source decompilers like ghidra and snowman are lackluster. however, there is not a single tutorial on the internet on how to actually write a machine code decompiler.
item.content:
wip…

preface

a large reason as to why i wanted to create a collection of notes, information, and references on decompilers is primarily due to the fact that its such an obscure craft.

i have personally never known of any class of software that was as difficult to find resources on as decompilers. hell even the documentation for open source decompilers like ghidra and snowman are lackluster.

however, there is not a single tutorial on the internet on how to actually write a machine code decompiler. you would think that there would be plenty of tutorial on this subject given the amount of compiler development tutorials that exist; but to this day i have not found any.

so ive decided that ill be the self proclaimed “first” to tackle such an obscure and complex piece of software, and hopefully write a coherent and self contained tutorial on decompiler development.

these are the notes and references that ive collected, if you want to view the tutorial, please view the “Decompiler Development” series within my posts (if they are available at the time of viewing this).

Resources

  • todo..

item.title: PGP

AwECHgECF4AACgkQ09zP/F7VeCLWCQ//YE+Jp2f64wrHoFvwklG+fXHq1/Wj8i3F 14iu7RA+BDiuWsnihSNcKEV9FY7+5u8Mor7FulfVUMQGPC7/JWUsW20iaM3/+Kna /t1l6+OMrslP2OZvxSwy4UFE2kJS33KyzxwIoTM94r/tgaG0K/155Ku1Js3HpMd1 bhvyBE7NWN3ugjUsvEerwmRxmGNF6kxqf7f839batRZAP3HWGTOlB0/9F6Mgvcck 2drRr3q0CUFESqXv/RjWkh4t/Fax6IMsBYpK/l2h+Kr5lZkuQxSaPNsMPKswx1mo ggdxD4ALuZLqW0rAs0NbW0+LsAYLGzcyzSpy/AeIvc9sIjAzMak98lTt7o5QNGv2 ihm3kg9BUPW/624HhFWYJZaK8AkATRZ4wfLcAUDf5i9MxXy5YA/5KK3rxCmiQnA6 2gqL1TYg+D16KBvdOTuS42EkcmmAV1ymhYSbOLZdZ6otB3qzOcJ1ZJnLBd3Tqp0K scrTUUm2p6Ggc+B75S42ICGcEOfhe72IrrKqkB1mnfBxXUQkuW9H/qNkcRcIkBOQ gy8YDYutcJ35eCfZr3kuub0JTXscEq3xBOvK690U8/Xlm4L5/L0Y3k+CR7Gf9EJw E8j8GR5YitAj8pv+/XxealJsBUX3d6BqY8ni5R94ROsjHUL7GJWHYVffEXTu+Q1u pDvCs8ZzkcK5Ag0EYtP9UwEQAM2QTVlnP8VrfFVUkSLhTkib0GwUiFpraNUiQlca item.title: Theory of Computation
item.content:
wip…

notes on the theory of computation

majority of these notes were taken throughout the course of reading sipser 3rd edition.

chomsky hierarchy

regular languages

context free languages

context sensitive languages

recursively enumerable languages

undecidability

determinism and nondeterminism

finite state automata

mealy and moore machines

push down automata

linear bound automata

the turing machine

incompletness

undecidability

the halting problem

complexity theory

item.title: Welcome
item.content:
Just testing shit out

header or something

testing out code formatting

int main(int argc, char**argv) {
  return 0;
}
